items.title: Abandonment Issues: Your Drupal Clients, and What Happens When You're Gone
items.full_description: Who cares what happens to that contract Drupal site after it launches? Our clients do, and we should too. Find out how to prepare your Drupal project for life after you leave: better prepping your clients for using Drupal without handholding, introducing them into a wider Drupal community, and designing code that someone -- perhaps you -- can inherit without wondering "what happened here?"
items.summary: Who cares what happens to that contract Drupal site after it launches?...

items.title: Domain Access: Managing multiple websites out of one Drupal install
items.full_description:
In this session we will go though the benefits we had from the implementation of a domain switching module for Drupal called Domain Access. This module allowed us to produce the perfect system to manage websites for any industry that has a head office > regional branch model.

Each branch has its own website, Head office can add content to any or all branches at once, while at the same time branches can add content to their own sites as well.
items.summary: In this session we will go though the benefits we had from the implementation of a domain switching module for Drupal called Domain Access....

items.title: PHP for Designers
items.full_description:
There are a lot of theme snippets available in the Theme Guide. There is not, however, a lot of information about PHP which is the language that makes up these snippets. If you're tired of copying, pasting and praying and are ready to understand some of the magic behind those snippets, this session is for you!

In this session you will learn how to manipulate and master:

* The very, very basics of PHP and the popular theming engine PHPtemplate

* Variables and tpl.php template files

* Arrays and objects and other crow-bar-worthy data containers

* The really scary looking stuff that's in the mysterious file template.php

Examples will be pulled from the Drupal.org Theme Guide as well as the wildly successful book on theming, Front End Drupal (co-authored by Emma Jane and Konstantin Kaefer).
items.summary: There are a lot of theme snippets available in the Theme Guide....

items.title: CiviCRM adventures in Drupal-land
items.full_description:
CiviCRM is an open source CRM built to work with Drupal, Joomla! or as standalone. It is a feature rich CRM that manages Events, Donations, Membership, Activities, Case management and more.

This workshop will run through the major features of CiviCRM and the risks and opportunities for closer relationships with Drupal through synching with Roles and OG, and integration with Views, CCK and Ubercart.
items.summary: CiviCRM is an open source CRM built to work with Drupal, Joomla!...

items.title: How and where does Drupal fit in the CMS market?
items.full_description:
The web CMS market is commonly segmented into 3 tiers:

* Top tier - large enterprise level proprietary CMS's such as Interwoven Team Site, Vignette Content Management Server, Stellent Web Content Management. These a very sophisticated products and are expensive to licence and implement. They are generally part of larger Enterprise Content Management suites including EDMS, EDRMS, Digital Asset Management, and collaboration components.

* Mid tier - Plone, Drupal, MySourceMatrix, Silverstripe, EpiServer, Kentico, Sitecore, Ektron, MOSS WCM. These have licence fees anywhere from $0 to $200k-ish, some are open source, and some are proprietary. They operate in the global market and have broad communities of vendors/implementers.

* Lower tier - Joomla, DotNetNuke, Wordpress and others. These are simpler products and are good for smaller sites (50 - 500 pages)

The mid-tier is currently the most dynamic part of the CMS market. For New Zealand it is the level that is suitable for most corporates and government agencies. It is still in its growth phase and is only just entering the consolidation phase that the top tier has been in for the last 3-4 years.

How does Drupal fit in the mid tier of the CMS market? How does it compare to the other mid-tier products (both FOSS and proprietary)? What are its strengths and weaknesses from a functional, technical, and sales point of view?

This session will answer these questions and will help those implementing and selling Drupal solutions learn how to better compete against other products and platforms.
items.summary: The web CMS market is commonly segmented into 3 tiers: Top tier - large enterprise level proprietary CMS's such as Interwoven Team Site, Vignette Content Management Server, Stellent Web Content Management....

items.title: (Long) Lunch - Finger Food (Included) LCA Open Day 11-2pm Wellington Conference Centre
"I am not a developer" - talking Drupal to clients
items.full_description:
Lots of my clients have said to me "I am not a developer!" - well, me neither.

Why do they think that matters, what are they afraid of, what do they want?

I'll run through some examples showing how clients see the world, how to handle the language gap, and a few key things we've found that make Drupal a dream to sell in and support.
items.summary: Lots of my clients have said to me "I am not a developer!" - well, me neither....

items.title: Blaming the unknown - constructive approach to technology
items.full_description:
If you don't know them, they will hurt you. No matter how expert you are, there are holes in your knowledge, and when things go wrong you usually blame what you know the least. So the culprit could be that database, the regular expression engine, the XML parser, the thread engine. What if the problem is between the chair and the keyboard instead?

This talk will give you some general insight on the art of software development, encouraging users to rant less and improve their own practice.
items.summary: If you don't know them, they will hurt you....

items.title: Using Drupal for collaborative historical research
items.full_description:
This case study will discuss the Founders and Survivors project, which brings together amateur historians and genealogists along with academics from many disciplines to collaborate in historical research. The presentation will cover some of the modules and techniques used to implement a data collection and verification system, and the challenges I have faced in making it accessible to users with minimal experience with computers.

 Attachment Size

 casestudy.pdf 1.75 MB
items.summary: This case study will discuss the Founders and Survivors project, which brings together amateur historians and genealogists along with academics from many disciplines to collaborate in historical research....

items.title: vetspace.org.nz
items.full_description: Launched in December 2008, VetSpace is the hub of information for the Veterinary Profession in New Zealand. VetSpace is a portal for at least a dozen websites in the 'family' and replaces a monolith built in Frontpage. During the RFP phase for the project it became apparent that Drupal was probably the only platform that would allow us to even get close to having a website with all the desired features. One of our key considerations was that it had to play with our proprietary CRM (iMIS) which involved syncing user data, event registrations, payments, membership subscriptions, and sales. It also need to talk with Moodle which we use to run online courses. We partnered with CatalystIT and Metisc (Perth based company) to do this development.
items.summary: Launched in December 2008, VetSpace is the hub of information for the Veterinary Profession in New Zealand....

items.title: "I am not a developer" - talking Drupal to clients
items.full_description:
Lots of my clients have said to me "I am not a developer!" - well, me neither.

Why do they think that matters, what are they afraid of, what do they want?

I'll run through some examples showing how clients see the world, how to handle the language gap, and a few key things we've found that make Drupal a dream to sell in and support.
items.summary: Lots of my clients have said to me "I am not a developer!" - well, me neither....

items.title: Multi-Master MySQL
items.full_description:
Implementing multi-master setups with MySQL is fairly trivial in basic terms, but for an effective environment much more is involved. There are some tools (such as MMM, Multi-Master MySQL Manager) that can help, but there's still a lot to think of and each deployment is different.

So why would you wish to bother, given all these complications? This session will discuss precisely that, as well as the tools and pitfalls.

Since each master has to execute the writes from the other anyway, so we're not doing it for write scaling. Sharding is a more effective way to handle that. Instead, multi-master MySQL can be used to ease failover, as well as enable maintenance tasks. It can deliver a system that, even with major upgrades or structural changes, does not require any down time. We all hate that "this website is undergoing maintenance", and it's bad for business. So let's avoid it.
items.summary: Implementing multi-master setups with MySQL is fairly trivial in basic terms, but for an effective environment much more is involved....

During the RFP phase for the project it became apparent that Drupal was probably the only platform that would allow us to even get close to having a website with all the desired features. One of our key considerations was that it had to play with our proprietary CRM (iMIS) which involved syncing user data, event registrations, payments, membership subscriptions, and sales. items.title: Looking Forward To Drupal 7
items.full_description:
This talk will provide an overview of the changes coming in the next release of Drupal, and how they will impact you as a site builder, designer, and/or developer.

Topics covered will include:

* Overview of Drupal 7 development cycle.

* New and exciting features to look forward to for end users, designers, and developers.

* Advice for those who wish to start porting their modules and themes.

* What's left to do before release, and how you (yes, you!) can help.

* Answers to frequently asked questions, such as "Should I start using Drupal 7 yet?"

While there will be some developer-focused material as part of the talk, everyone is encouraged to attend.
items.summary: This talk will provide an overview of the changes coming in the next release of Drupal, and how they will impact you as a site builder, designer, and/or developer....

items.title: Drupal hosting in the clouds
items.full_description:
There is a lot of talk about "the cloud" these days. But what benefits and possibilities does this offer for those who need to run a Drupal site? You get "a cloud" when you replace hardware with software. Cloning a server has now become as easy as making a copy of it. Other benefits might not be so immediately apparent, so this hands-on demo will demonstrate what possibilities the cloud offers for hosting in general and Drupal hosting in particular?

The session briefly starts with what still works, and what doesn't. Next it delves into the entirely new possibilities that the cloud offers. The session will answer how reliable the cloud, how to increase up-time and how to build, or gradually make, your environment more scalable.

Demonstrations will be based upon Amazon's EC2 cloud offering, and discuss the particular features EC2 has. It is not a getting started with EC2, as there are enough resources on this, but a demonstration of how EC2 can be used in various scenarios.

The session is intended for those who already host Drupal or who want to host Drupal and are not sure if the cloud is something for them.
items.summary: There is a lot of talk about "the cloud" these days....

items.title: Lightning Talks / Ignite Talks
items.full_description:
Action-packed, info-filled, mind-bending fun. Lightning talks are super-short presentations.

We'll divide our session of 45 minutes by the number of people who sign up to give a Lightning Talk , and that's how many minutes short each talk will be. That includes set up time, so if you have slides it's a good idea to get them on to the presentation laptop in advance to maximise your speaking time.

But what can you possibly cover in 3-5 minutes? Quite a lot. But the point is to cover the basics, demonstrate one key idea, pose a question, or promote a project or event.

Sign up on the day. Keep it brief . Strike like lightning.
items.summary: Action-packed, info-filled, mind-bending fun....

items.title: Tidy-up: we could do with everyone lending a hand as the function room is booked for this evening so we can't leave it set up.
Module Development: or How I Learned to Stop Worrying and Love the Module
items.full_description:
This session will give an introduction to module development for those who fear the module, or specifically getting knee deep in code and writing your own. It will introduce the basic concepts in building a Drupal module and will introduce the concept of hooks to help take knowledge gained from the session further.

During the session, we will build a module that will display a number of simple blocks and demonstrate how to build an administration/configuration page.

The session is a moderately technical session, aimed at those who know their way round Drupal, have PHP skills, have browsed the API and done some work in template.php.

Co-presented by: Alastair Moore and Paul Flewelling.
items.summary: This session will give an introduction to module development for those who fear the module, or specifically getting knee deep in code and writing your own....

items.title: Ubercart - converting your drupal site into a money machine.
items.full_description:
This session will first introduce the various Ubercart modules and the basic steps required to setup a simple ecommerce store on a Drupal site.

But shopping carts are rather boring and those types of business require lots of off-line work (e.g. handling stock). There are other more interesting and fun ways to turn your drupal site into a money machine.

One of these is running a subscription/membership service and having your site automatically charge users every month. So we will step through the basic site recipe for setting up a paid membership site using Ubercart.
items.summary: This session will first introduce the various Ubercart modules and the basic steps required to setup a simple ecommerce store on a Drupal site....

items.title: Doesn't Play Nicely!
items.full_description:
Modules interact with Drupal's various objects as they pass through Drupal space, and those objects can be adjusted by each module in turn. Of course, many modules add or interact with object attributes which aren't in core.

How do we make sure that those modules will play nicely together? What happens if they don't?

How can you tell if your module is interacting with other modules as they expect?

Core functionality is well documented and this makes it clear when a module misbehaves, but with contrib modules this isn't typically the case. Is documentation a realistic solution here?
items.summary: Modules interact with Drupal's various objects as they pass through Drupal space, and those objects can be adjusted by each module in turn....

items.title: FOSS: The We Economy
items.full_description:
For nearly ten years I have used open source software tools to earn a living. It may have started as the "me" economy, but over the last decade I have become part of the "we" economy. I rely on complete strangers to build me the tools that I use daily; and other complete strangers rely on the work that I do too. I have deployed open source solutions for government agencies, non-profits and for-profit companies. Everyone wants to know, "What's the catch?"

In this 45 minute presentation we'll explore the underbelly of the open source world and address the questions:

* Is it a scam? What about my competitor advantage? Can they steal my password?

* Who uses this stuff anyway? (Hint: everyone in this room is probably using something that was built on open source software.)

* Can serious for-profit businesses (and government) rely on this "free" stuff?

* Who makes this stuff? They can't possibly have a job. They must live in their mother's basement!

FOSS is serious business and it works because people like you pitch in. In the second half of the presentation I'll give you a tour of some of the contributions I've made to the FOSS community (hint: very few of them are programming). I'll wrap up with my 2010 project: The 100 Mile Client Roster. In this project I will be open sourcing my business so that you can do what I do too.*

(* Book deals, international fame, and world travel not guaranteed.)
items.summary: For nearly ten years I have used open source software tools to earn a living....

items.title: Building interactive calculators
items.full_description: A brief introduction to building online calculators with Drupal using CCK and some custom code. http://acoustics.nzta.govt.nz/node/add/calc-road-noise
items.summary: A brief introduction to building online calculators with Drupal using CCK and some custom code....

items.title: Drupal for Project Management - lessons learned
items.full_description:
At last year's DrupalSouth, I spoke about "Ned", Egressive's internal project management system. This talk provides an update to the story.

Egressive is now actively managing our development projects and tracking staff and contract developer time using "Ned 2" on Drupal 6. In addition to providing Egressive with tools to manage our development resources in realtime, and build invoices based on actual time worked on customer tasks, customer representatives can log in and submit new tasks and refer to existing tasks and time against them.

I'll be showing some examples of its use and discussing some of the implications as well as future plans.
items.summary: At last year's DrupalSouth, I spoke about "Ned", Egressive's internal project management system....

items.title: fixmystreet.org.nz
items.full_description: Background to the http://fixmystreet.org.nz project, using Drupal to emulate FixMyStreet.com touching geo, iphone, APIs, workflow and more.
items.summary: Background to the http://fixmystreet.org.nz project, using Drupal to emulate FixMyStreet.com touching geo, iphone, APIs, workflow and more....

items.title: Training Wheels - Learning and Teaching Drupal
items.full_description:
Come along to discuss the state of Drupal training.

Let's explore questions like:

 - Who needs training? users? designers? developers? usability experts? security specialists? sys-admins?

 - What does best practice look like?

 - When will the Acquia / Drupal Association certification program be ready?

 - Where are the resources? Can we share them? Can we build on them?

 - How can we work together to train our users and colleagues, and extend our own skills and knowledge?

 - Can we all help build a resource bank of training materials available under an open license so the community can contribute and develop a collaborative and modular course design that's flexible and adaptable, and able to be freely translated into other languages?

 - What does the Drupal learning curve look like? What skills do people need? Can they adapt skills from other CMS's?

 - Can we develop comparison rubrics so people can map their existing knowledge against the Drupal skill set?

This won't be a 'sage on the stage' presentation, because I'm no expert. Instead, I aim to facilitate an interactive discussion and invite all attendees to contribute their thoughts, experience and wisdom. We'll use some form of knowledge capture during the session to record and summarise the conversation so a post event report can be generated and shared with the Drupal community.

 Link to slides on external site: See slides for Training Wheels - Learning and Teaching Drupal (external link) Link to slides on external site: See slides for Training Wheels - Learning and Teaching Drupal (external link)
items.summary: Come along to discuss the state of Drupal training....

items.title: jQuery Basics Plus Plus
items.full_description:
Small non-intrusive animations and interactions can greatly enhance user experience and impress website visitors and clients. jQuery and jQuery UI make it easy to add these enhancements with little or no knowledge of javascript. Learn how! Or if you already know some jQuery come and learn something you didn't already know!

This session will be based on a similar session I gave at DrupalCon Paris and the related jQuery for Designers and Themers theme for Drupal 6. Though, depending on the audience, we may move over basics more quickly to spend more time on the " plus plus " topics.

No understanding of jQuery, javascript or even Drupal theming is required, but a good understanding of CSS selectors (e.g. " #page #content > p a.read_more {} ") and how HTML elements are nested to make a tree (a.k.a. DOM) is expected.
items.summary: Small non-intrusive animations and interactions can greatly enhance user experience and impress website visitors and clients....

items.title: MySQL > YourSQL
items.full_description:
Most distributions ship MySQL with a configuration file that sees you running a server optimised for a system with 32MB of RAM. That was great in 1996, but these days your Nike+ shoes have more capacity than that, so it makes sense to optimise the configuration.

Are you using a stock MySQL installation to power your Drupals? Find out why you should switch to using the InnoDB storage engine and how to make it not suck with just a few basic tweaks.

 Link to slides on external site: See slides for MySQL > YourSQL (external link) Link to slides on external site: See slides for MySQL > YourSQL (external link)
items.summary: Most distributions ship MySQL with a configuration file that sees you running a server optimised for a system with 32MB of RAM....

items.title: Easy and Fast Tree/Graph Traversal and Social Networking in Drupal
items.full_description:
Everybody knows that dealing with hierarchies in a relational database is awkward, and depending on your needs it gets ugly, or downright impossible to do quickly in SQL (lots of queries needed, etc).

What if...

- you could do such operations with simple single queries?

- you could have not only simple trees but also complex ones (multiple parents) ?

- you could have complex graphs for friend-of-a-friend or other structures?

GRAPH engine does all that, without breaking a sweat. Externally it looks like a table and a storage engine, but internally it's a computational engine, in this case specialised for graph operations.

It's GPLv2, and available now for MySQL 5.0 and 5.1
items.summary: Everybody knows that dealing with hierarchies in a relational database is awkward, and depending on your needs it gets ugly, or downright impossible to do quickly in SQL (lots of queries needed, etc)....

items.title: jQuery Basics Plus Plus
items.full_description:
Small non-intrusive animations and interactions can greatly enhance user experience and impress website visitors and clients. jQuery and jQuery UI make it easy to add these enhancements with little or no knowledge of javascript. Learn how! Or if you already know some jQuery come and learn something you didn't already know!

This session will be based on a similar session I gave at DrupalCon Paris and the related jQuery for Designers and Themers theme for Drupal 6. Though, depending on the audience, we may move over basics more quickly to spend more time on the " plus plus " topics.

No understanding of jQuery, javascript or even Drupal theming is required, but a good understanding of CSS selectors (e.g. " #page #content > p a.read_more {} ") and how HTML elements are nested to make a tree (a.k.a. DOM) is expected.
items.summary: Small non-intrusive animations and interactions can greatly enhance user experience and impress website visitors and clients....

items.title: Boost database performance with MySQL partitions
items.full_description:
Databases always grow. With partitions, MySQL 5.1 becomes an efficient and easy to use data warehouse. This session explains with practical examples how to create and use MySQL partitions and how they will increase your application performance. This presentation also covers the recent additions from MySQL 5.5

 Link to slides on external site: See slides for Boost database performance with MySQL partitions (external link)
items.summary: Databases always grow....

items.title: Drupal.net.nz site re-build in 7
items.full_description:
Let's help the Drupal community as it launches the latest and greatest core feature set we call version 7, by taking it for a collaborative spin.

At the same time serving our kiwi Drupal community by re-building our Drupal showcase site, currently in D5.

Be part of the process of brain-storming, finding the right man (or woman) for the job, squashing bugs, contributing patches, and simply getting heads down and bums up so it gets done.

No idle chitter chatter here mates.

Are you up to the challenge?

We are looking for an over-arching development team who are conversant in the inner workings of D.o to mentor others in the deployment, bug-squishing, patching process, as well as getting down and dirty themselves.

There is also a call for a team leader in the theming, wow and pizzaz department.

Please leave your ideas on what you'd like to see on New Zealand's Drupal Showcase site in the comments below.
items.summary: Let's help the Drupal community as it launches the latest and greatest core feature set we call version 7, by taking it for a collaborative spin....

items.title: Config in code with Drush and Features
items.full_description:
Configurations in Drupal is what defines your website and makes it different from other Drupal sites. Because most of the configurations are stored in the database, it can become a challenge to keep these configurations consistent between development, staging and production environments.

Drush and Features are two great tools that can help create, deploy and manage the the different configuration sets that make up the features of your site.

This session will cover how to use Drush and Features to compile a set of features and deploy them from development to staging to production environments also with the aid of Git version control.
items.summary: Configurations in Drupal is what defines your website and makes it different from other Drupal sites....

items.title: Aegir
items.full_description:
Dave Hall will discuss what aegir is, why you should use it and how he uses it. Aegir allow system administrators (and developers) to quickly and easily manage the deployment and updating of their Drupal sites. If you are running more than 10 or so drupal sites, you probably should be running them in a multi site instance with aegir, if you don't believe me, you should be convinced by the end of this session.

Dave has been using aegir since the 0.2 alphas to deploy and manage the largest know production multisite instance of Drupal with almost 2100 sites running off one code base.

Miguel Jacq (aka mig5) was meant to co present this talk, but he piked out.
items.summary: Dave Hall will discuss what aegir is, why you should use it and how he uses it....

items.title: Job Fair, Wrap-up and Pack Down
